title: Iranian Leaders Unite to Reject Trump's Claims of a Rift: 'We Are One Soul, One Nation'
summary: For the past week, President Donald Trump and his aides have been repeating a narrative: Iran's leadership is deeply divided, with "moderates" and "hardliners" locked in a power struggle. Trump even claimed that Iranians are "having a very hard time figuring out who their leader is." But on Thursday, Iran's top officials fired back with a unified message that was impossible to ignore. Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, and Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf all posted the exact same statement on X (formerly Twitter). "In Iran, there are no radicals or moderates," it read. "We are all 'Iranian' and 'revolutionary,' and with the iron unity of the nation and government, with complete obedience to the Supreme Leader of the Revolution, we will make the aggressor criminal regret his actions." First Vice President Mohammad Reza Aref added in English: "Iran is not a land of rifts, but a stronghold of unity. We are one soul, one nation." This show of unity comes at a critical moment. Iran's former Supreme Leader, Ali Khamenei, was killed in U.S.-Israeli strikes on February 28. His son, Mojtaba Khamenei, has not been seen in public since. Meanwhile, the U.S. has imposed a naval blockade on Iranian ports, and Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz — a narrow waterway crucial for global oil shipments. Oil prices are rising as a result. Trump boasted on social media that the blockade is "airtight" and that Iran's navy and air force are "lying at the bottom of the sea." He also warned that U.S. forces would "shoot and kill" any Iranian laying mines in the Strait of Hormuz. Israel's Defense Minister, Israel Katz, said his country is waiting for Trump's green light to "return Iran to the age of darkness." But Iran insists it is united and ready to defend itself. Araghchi dismissed claims of a military-political rift, saying, "The battlefield and diplomacy are fully coordinated fronts in the same war. Iranians are all united, more than ever before."
why_it_matters: This standoff affects global oil prices, international diplomacy, and the risk of a wider war — issues that could impact your family's budget and the world's stability for years to come.

keywords.explanation: Iranian officials who might favor negotiation, as claimed by Trump
Iranian officials who oppose compromise, as claimed by Trump
the highest authority in Iran, currently Mojtaba Khamenei
a U.S. naval blockade that prevents ships from reaching Iranian ports
narrow waterway for global oil shipments, closed by Iran
the message Iranian leaders sent to deny any internal rift

questions.question: Who posted the exact same statement on X?
options: President, foreign minister, and parliament speaker
questions.correct_answer: President, foreign minister, and parliament speaker

questions.question: What did Trump claim about Iran's navy and air force?
options: They are lying at the bottom of the sea
questions.correct_answer: They are lying at the bottom of the sea

questions.question: What did Israel’s Defense Minister say they are waiting for?
Who added the English statement: 'We are one soul, one nation'?
options: First Vice President Mohammad Reza Aref
questions.correct_answer: First Vice President Mohammad Reza Aref

questions.question: What did Araghchi say about the battlefield and diplomacy?
options: They are fully coordinated fronts in the same war
The battlefield is the only option
questions.correct_answer: They are fully coordinated fronts in the same war

background_read: Iran and the United States have had a hostile relationship since the 1979 Iranian Revolution and the hostage crisis. Tensions escalated under Trump's first term when he pulled out of the 2015 nuclear deal and imposed sanctions. The killing of Qasem Soleimani in 2020 brought the two countries close to open war.

The Strait of Hormuz is a geopolitical chokepoint: about 20% of the world's oil passes through it. Iran has threatened to close it in previous crises, most notably during the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988).

The phrase 'moderates vs. hardliners' is a common Western framing of Iranian politics, but many Iranians reject it as oversimplified. The death of Ali Khamenei, who was Supreme Leader for 35 years, created a power vacuum. His son Mojtaba Khamenei now leads, but his lack of public appearances has fueled uncertainty.

The CFTC (Commodity Futures Trading Commission) does not appear in this article, so no need to include it. The article cites Israel’s Defense Minister Israel Katz, a far-right figure known for aggressive rhetoric toward Iran.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Trump and aides have claimed Iran's leadership is divided, but Iran's officials responded with a unified message.
└─ specific: 'President Masoud Pezeshkian, Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, and Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf all posted the exact same statement on X'
KEY DEVELOPMENTS: Show of unity; details of the statement.
├─ CONTENT: 'In Iran, there are no radicals or moderates... We are all Iranian and revolutionary.'
├─ ADDED BY VICE PRESIDENT: 'Iran is not a land of rifts... We are one soul, one nation.'
CONTEXT: Recent events that set the stage – death of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei on Feb 28.
├─ NEW LEADER: Mojtaba Khamenei, not seen in public.
├─ U.S. NAVAL BLOCKADE on Iranian ports
└─ IRAN CLOSED STRAIT OF HORMUZ – oil prices rising
TENSION: Trump’s response vs Iran’s claim of unity.
├─ TRUMP: Blockade 'airtight', Iranian navy 'lying at the bottom of the sea', threatens to shoot miners.
├─ ISRAEL KATZ: Waiting for Trump’s green light to 'return Iran to the age of darkness'.
└─ IRAN’S ARAGHCHI: 'battlefield and diplomacy are fully coordinated fronts... Iranians are all united, more than ever before.'
OPEN QUESTION: Is Iran truly united, or is the public message covering up real divisions? The next moves of Trump and Israel will test the claim.

perspectives.description: Iranian leadership benefits from the unified message as it projects strength and discourages internal dissent. The show of unity may also rally the population against a foreign adversary, boosting morale during a difficult time. For the global audience, it presents Iran as a coherent actor rather than a fractured regime.

The U.S. and Israel are harmed because their narrative of a divided Iran is publicly contradicted, weakening the justification for further strikes. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz harms the global economy, especially countries reliant on Middle East oil. Civilians in Iran face worsened economic conditions due to the blockade and rising oil prices.

This situation echoes previous patterns of brinkmanship: Iran often issues defiant statements during crises to signal unity, while the U.S. escalates military pressure. The death of a long-serving Supreme Leader creates inevitable instability, but whether internal divisions actually exist is hard to verify from outside. The coordinated statement suggests at least a temporary tactical consensus among top officials.

The next few weeks will be critical. If Israel strikes Iran with U.S. approval, the region could plunge into a wider war. Alternatively, diplomacy might reopen if both sides back down. Iran’s internal unity will be tested if the new Supreme Leader fails to appear publicly or if economic pressure causes unrest. The global oil market will remain volatile as long as the Strait of Hormuz is closed.
